tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Use vector aligned instructions:
  1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries
  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 6 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
2 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
5 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 12 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 6: addition or subtraction
 - 6: multiply
The binary loop is loading 64 bytes (8 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 32 bytes (4 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.12 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.title: General properties
expert.txt:
nb instructions    : 17
nb uops            : 16
loop length        : 71
used x86 registers : 6
used mmx registers : 0
used xmm registers : 5
used ymm registers : 0
used zmm registers : 0
nb stack references: 0
ADD-SUB / MUL ratio: 1.33

expert.title: Front-end
expert.txt:
ASSUMED MACRO FUSION
FIT IN UOP CACHE
micro-operation queue: 2.67 cycles
front end            : 2.67 cycles

expert.help: Back-end corresponds to macro/micro operations execution (includes allocating registers, fetching operands etc.)
expert.title: Back-end
expert.txt:
| ALU0/BRU0 | ALU1 | ALU2 | ALU3/BRU1 | AGU0 | AGU1 | AGU2 | FP0  | FP1  | FP2  | FP3
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
uops   | 0.50      | 0.50 | 0.50 | 0.50      | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 3.00 | 3.00
cycles | 0.50      | 0.50 | 0.50 | 0.50      | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 2.00 | 3.00 | 3.00

Execution ports to units layout:
 - ALU0/BRU0: ALU, BRU
 - ALU1: ALU
 - ALU2: ALU
 - ALU3/BRU1: ALU, BRU
 - AGU0 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU1 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU2: store address
 - FP0 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP1 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP2 (256 bits): VPU, FP store data
 - FP3 (256 bits): VPU, DIV/SQRT

Cycles executing div or sqrt instructions: NA
Cycles loading/storing data              : 2.00
Longest recurrence chain latency (RecMII): 6.00

expert.title: Cycles summary
expert.txt:
Front-end : 2.67
Dispatch  : 3.00
Data deps.: 6.00
Overall L1: 6.00

expert.help: Proportion of vectorizable instructions that are actually vectorized. Higher is better.

expert.txt:

store   : 100%
mul     : 100%
add-sub : 50%
fma     : NA (no fma vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 0%

expert.title: Vector efficiency ratios
expert.txt:
all     : 44%
load    : 50%
store   : 50%
mul     : 50%
add-sub : 37%
fma     : NA (no fma vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 25%

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 6.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 16% of peak load performance is reached (10.67 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 16% of peak store performance is reached (5.33 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 8% of peak computational performance is used (2.00 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
78% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 44% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 6.00 to 2.47 cycles (2.43x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt: Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

hint.workaround:

  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Use vector aligned instructions:

  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.
